QF.id: IV Fixed Income Instruments and Bond Markets_48

question.text: An NBFC has issued inverse floater bonds linked to MIBOR with a coupon formula of '10% - MIBOR spread'. If MIBOR rises from 5% to 7%, the NBFC's coupon cost:

explanation.text:
**The Logic:** Coupon = 10% - MIBOR spread. When MIBOR is 5%, coupon = 10% - 5% = 5%. When MIBOR rises to 7%, coupon = 10% - 7% = 3%. The NBFC's interest cost DECREASES as rates rise — this is the defining feature of inverse floaters.
**The Trap:** Regular floaters would increase cost with rising rates. Inverse floaters move opposite to rates — beneficial for issuers when rates rise, but costly when rates fall.
**Cross-Exam Context:** Inverse floater mechanics are tested in Series IV and demonstrate how structured products can help issuers manage interest rate risk.
**Distributor's Pro-Tip:** Inverse floaters are double-edged — if MIBOR rises above 10%, the coupon would be negative (usually floored at 0%). Always check for floor provisions in the indenture.
